message.content:
You are a senior applied cryptographer doing a DEEP second-pass audit of Coinbase cb-mpc. A first broad pass found nothing; go deeper and more exhaustive on this narrow surface. Rigor over volume.
REPO ROOT: C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc. Read the named files IN FULL; follow into include/cbmpc/** and include-internal/** for definitions. Anchor every finding to a public API entry (pve_base_pke.h, pve_batch_single_recipient.h).

BOUNTY SCOPE: eligible only if reachable via public APIs in include/cbmpc/api/. demo-* and include/cbmpc/c_api/* are OUT OF SCOPE as entry points. Primitive/ZK bugs eligible only when tied to a named public-API path. Medium+ needs an independent-parties PoC (>=1 honest party on unmodified code; malicious party interacts only through the public protocol boundary). Tiers: Critical $50k (key compromise/RCE), High $15k, Medium $2k, Low $200.

SURFACE: Publicly Verifiable Encryption verification soundness. Read src/cbmpc/protocol/pve.cpp, pve_base.cpp, pve_batch.cpp IN FULL plus include/cbmpc/api/pve_base_pke.h and pve_batch_single_recipient.h. Core question: can a malicious encryptor craft (ciphertext, proof) that PASSES verification but decrypts to a DIFFERENT or zero/garbage plaintext than committed (soundness break => key-backup/escrow exposure or loss)? Check binding between the verified statement and what decryption actually returns, and completeness of the proof checks.

Build an explicit mental table: received-value -> is it validated? where? if not, attack. Report ONLY values used without a required check that a malicious party can exploit with honest counterparties on unmodified code.
At most 5 findings, best-first; ZERO is a fully acceptable, honest result. Each finding: public_api_entry, exact file:line, the precise missing_check, data_flow with function names, attacker_role, impact, poc_sketch through the public API, honest confidence in [0,1].
In coverage_notes, briefly list the received values you DID confirm are properly validated (evidence the pass was thorough). Return ONLY the structured object.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the named files in full.

content.content:
1	#pragma once
2	
3	#include <cbmpc/api/curve.h>
4	#include <cbmpc/core/buf.h>
5	#include <cbmpc/core/error.h>
6	
7	namespace coinbase::api::pve {
8	
9	// Pluggable base public-key encryption (PKE) used by PVE.
10	//
11	// The PVE construction requires *deterministic* encryption given `rho`:
12	// calling `encrypt(...)` with the same inputs (including the same `rho`) must
13	// produce the same ciphertext.
14	//
15	// Implementations must treat `rho` as the sole source of encryption randomness.
16	class base_pke_i {
17	 public:
18	  virtual ~base_pke_i() = default;
19	
20	  // Encrypt `plain` under `ek` with associated data `label`, using `rho` as
21	  // deterministic randomness seed.
22	  virtual error_t encrypt(mem_t ek, mem_t label, mem_t plain, mem_t rho, buf_t& out_ct) const = 0;
23	
24	  // Decrypt `ct` under `dk` with associated data `label`.
25	  virtual error_t decrypt(mem_t dk, mem_t label, mem_t ct, buf_t& out_plain) const = 0;
26	};
27	
28	// Built-in base PKE implementation (default).
29	//
30	// Key format:
31	// - `ek` / `dk` are opaque, versioned byte strings generated by:
32	//   - `generate_base_pke_rsa_keypair(...)`
33	//   - `generate_base_pke_ecies_p256_keypair(...)`
34	//
35	// Notes:
36	// - The built-in base PKE supports RSA-OAEP(2048) and ECIES(P-256).
37	// - The base PKE is independent of the curve used by the outer PVE scheme.
38	const base_pke_i& base_pke_default();
39	
40	// Generate a keypair for the built-in RSA-OAEP (2048-bit) base PKE.
41	//
42	// Outputs are opaque, versioned byte strings compatible with `base_pke_default()`.
43	error_t generate_base_pke_rsa_keypair(buf_t& out_ek, buf_t& out_dk);
44	
45	// Generate a keypair for the built-in ECIES (P-256) base PKE.
46	//
47	// Outputs are opaque, versioned byte strings compatible with `base_pke_default()`.
48	error_t generate_base_pke_ecies_p256_keypair(buf_t& out_ek, buf_t& out_dk);
49	
50	// Build a built-in ECIES(P-256) base PKE public key blob from an external public key.
51	//
52	// This is useful when the private key lives in an HSM (or other external system)
53	// and only the public key can be exported to software.
54	//
55	// Input format:
56	// - `pub_key_oct` must be the *uncompressed* NIST P-256 public key octet string:
57	//   65 bytes: 0x04 || X(32) || Y(32).
58	//
59	// Output:
60	// - `out_ek` is an opaque, versioned byte string compatible with:
61	//   - `base_pke_default()`
62	//   - `encrypt/verify(...)`
63	//   - `decrypt_ecies_p256_hsm(...)`
64	error_t base_pke_ecies_p256_ek_from_oct(mem_t pub_key_oct, buf_t& out_ek);
65	
66	// Build a built-in RSA-OAEP(2048) base PKE public key blob from a raw modulus.
67	//
68	// This is useful when the private key lives in an HSM (or other external system)
69	// that exports only the raw modulus (e.g. YubiHSM 2).
70	//
71	// Input format:
72	// - `modulus` must be the big-endian RSA modulus (256 bytes for RSA-2048).
73	// - The public exponent is assumed to be 65537.
74	//
75	// Output:
76	// - `out_ek` is an opaque, versioned byte string compatible with:
77	//   - `base_pke_default()`
78	//   - `encrypt/verify(...)`
79	//   - `decrypt_rsa_oaep_hsm(...)`
80	error_t base_pke_rsa_ek_from_modulus(mem_t modulus, buf_t& out_ek);
81	
82	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
83	// Built-in HSM support (KEM decapsulation callback)
84	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
85	
86	// RSA-OAEP decapsulation callback.
87	//
88	// This callback is responsible only for recovering the RSA KEM shared secret
89	// from `kem_ct` using an HSM-held private key referenced by `dk_handle`.
90	// The library performs HKDF + AES-GCM DEM and ciphertext parsing.
91	struct rsa_oaep_hsm_decap_cb_t {
92	  void* ctx = nullptr;
93	  error_t (*decap)(void* ctx, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t kem_ct, buf_t& out_kem_ss) = nullptr;
94	};
95	
96	// ECIES(P-256) ECDH callback.
97	//
98	// This callback should perform the ECDH operation using the HSM-held private
99	// key referenced by `dk_handle` and the ephemeral public key encoded in `kem_ct`.
100	// It must output the raw affine-X coordinate as a 32-byte big-endian buffer.
101	// The library derives the final KEM shared secret and performs HKDF + AES-GCM DEM.
102	struct ecies_p256_hsm_ecdh_cb_t {
103	  void* ctx = nullptr;
104	  error_t (*ecdh)(void* ctx, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t kem_ct, buf_t& out_dh_x32) = nullptr;
105	};
106	
107	// Encrypt a scalar `x` under base encryption key `ek`, producing a PVE ciphertext.
108	//
109	// - `curve` selects the elliptic curve used for the outer PVE relation (Q = x*G).
110	// - `label` is associated data that must match in `verify()` / `decrypt()`.
111	// - `x` is interpreted as a big-endian integer and reduced modulo the curve order.
112	// - Defensive limit: `x.size` must be <= the curve order size in bytes.
113	error_t encrypt(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t label, mem_t x, buf_t& out_ciphertext);
114	
115	// Same as `encrypt(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
116	error_t encrypt(curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t label, mem_t x, buf_t& out_ciphertext);
117	
118	// Verify ciphertext validity against the expected public value Q and label.
119	//
120	// - `Q_compressed` is the compressed public point encoding for `curve`
121	//   (33 bytes for P-256/secp256k1, 32 bytes for Ed25519).
122	error_t verify(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t Q_compressed, mem_t label);
123	
124	// Same as `verify(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
125	error_t verify(curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t Q_compressed, mem_t label);
126	
127	// Decrypt a ciphertext, recovering the scalar x.
128	//
129	// Output:
130	// - On success, `out_x` is set to a fixed-length big-endian encoding of x with
131	//   length equal to the curve order size in bytes (e.g., 32 bytes for P-256).
132	//
133	// Notes:
134	// - This function intentionally does not verify `ciphertext` before decryption.
135	//   Invalid ciphertexts may cause decryption to fail, but are designed to not
136	//   leak secret information.
137	// - If you need ciphertext validation, call `verify(...)` first.
138	error_t decrypt(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t dk, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
139	                buf_t& out_x);
140	
141	// Same as `decrypt(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
142	error_t decrypt(curve_id curve, mem_t dk, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label, buf_t& out_x);
143	
144	// Decrypt using an HSM-backed RSA private key.
145	//
146	// - `dk_handle` is an opaque handle (or identifier) understood by the caller's HSM callback.
147	// - `ek` is the software public key blob (used for verification and encryption-side checks).
148	error_t decrypt_rsa_oaep_hsm(curve_id curve, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
149	                             const rsa_oaep_hsm_decap_cb_t& cb, buf_t& out_x);
150	
151	// Decrypt using an HSM-backed ECIES(P-256) private key.
152	//
153	// - `dk_handle` is an opaque handle (or identifier) understood by the caller's HSM callback.
154	// - `ek` is the software public key blob (used for verification and to derive the KEM context).
155	error_t decrypt_ecies_p256_hsm(curve_id curve, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
156	                               const ecies_p256_hsm_ecdh_cb_t& cb, buf_t& out_x);
157	
158	// Extract the public value Q whose discrete log is encrypted as the ciphertext, returning compressed point encoding.
159	error_t get_public_key_compressed(mem_t ciphertext, buf_t& out_Q_compressed);
160	
161	// Extract the associated label from a ciphertext.
162	error_t get_Label(mem_t ciphertext, buf_t& out_label);
163	
164	}  // namespace coinbase::api::pve
165

1	#pragma once
2	
3	#include <vector>
4	
5	#include <cbmpc/api/curve.h>
6	#include <cbmpc/api/pve_base_pke.h>
7	#include <cbmpc/core/buf.h>
8	#include <cbmpc/core/error.h>
9	
10	namespace coinbase::api::pve {
11	
12	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
13	// Batch PVE (1P) API
14	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
15	//
16	// This API batches the core PVE algorithm for multiple scalars {x_i} in one
17	// ciphertext, sharing the same label and base PKE key.
18	//
19	// Notes:
20	// - `xs[i]` is interpreted as a big-endian integer and reduced modulo the curve order.
21	// - Defensive limit: each `xs[i].size` must be <= the curve order size in bytes.
22	// - `Qs_compressed[i]` is the compressed point encoding for the outer curve:
23	//   33 bytes for P-256/secp256k1, 32 bytes for Ed25519.
24	
25	error_t encrypt_batch(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t label, const std::vector<mem_t>& xs,
26	                      buf_t& out_ciphertext);
27	
28	// Same as `encrypt_batch(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
29	error_t encrypt_batch(curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t label, const std::vector<mem_t>& xs, buf_t& out_ciphertext);
30	
31	error_t verify_batch(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext,
32	                     const std::vector<mem_t>& Qs_compressed, mem_t label);
33	
34	// Same as `verify_batch(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
35	error_t verify_batch(curve_id curve, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, const std::vector<mem_t>& Qs_compressed, mem_t label);
36	
37	// Decrypt a batch ciphertext, recovering the vector of scalars {x_i}.
38	//
39	// Output:
40	// - On success, `out_xs[i]` is a fixed-length big-endian encoding of x_i with
41	//   length equal to the curve order size in bytes (e.g., 32 bytes for P-256).
42	//
43	// Notes:
44	// - This function intentionally does not verify `ciphertext` before decryption.
45	//   Invalid ciphertexts may cause decryption to fail, but are designed to not
46	//   leak secret information.
47	// - If you need ciphertext validation, call `verify_batch(...)` first.
48	error_t decrypt_batch(const base_pke_i& base_pke, curve_id curve, mem_t dk, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
49	                      std::vector<buf_t>& out_xs);
50	
51	// Same as `decrypt_batch(...)` using `base_pke_default()`.
52	error_t decrypt_batch(curve_id curve, mem_t dk, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label, std::vector<buf_t>& out_xs);
53	
54	// Decrypt a batch ciphertext using an HSM-backed RSA private key.
55	error_t decrypt_batch_rsa_oaep_hsm(curve_id curve, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
56	                                   const rsa_oaep_hsm_decap_cb_t& cb, std::vector<buf_t>& out_xs);
57	
58	// Decrypt a batch ciphertext using an HSM-backed ECIES(P-256) private key.
59	error_t decrypt_batch_ecies_p256_hsm(curve_id curve, mem_t dk_handle, mem_t ek, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label,
60	                                     const ecies_p256_hsm_ecdh_cb_t& cb, std::vector<buf_t>& out_xs);
61	
62	// Extract batch count from a batch ciphertext.
63	error_t get_batch_count(mem_t ciphertext, int& out_batch_count);
64	
65	// Extract the public values {Q_i} whose discrete logs are encrypted as the ciphertext, returning compressed point
66	// encodings.
67	error_t get_public_keys_compressed_batch(mem_t ciphertext, std::vector<buf_t>& out_Qs_compressed);
68	
69	// Extract the associated label from a batch ciphertext.
70	error_t get_Label_batch(mem_t ciphertext, buf_t& out_label);
71	
72	}  // namespace coinbase::api::pve
73

content.text: Now let me read pve_base.cpp and pve_batch.cpp, plus the internal header.

Now let me analyze the binding carefully. The key soundness question: does `verify` guarantee that decryption returns the committed `x` (i.e., `Q`'s discrete log)?

Key observation in `restore_from_decrypted` (both base and batch): after computing `x_value`, it checks `x_value * G == Q`. This is the final binding check that catches inconsistencies. So even if verify passes, decryption itself re-checks against Q. Let me examine the batch hash bitlen discrepancy I noticed: encrypt uses `.bitlen(kappa)` (line 63) but verify uses `.bitlen(SEC_P_COM)` (line 139). Let me check if kappa == SEC_P_COM.

input.command: cd /c/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc && grep -rn "SEC_P_COM\|SEC_P_STAT\b" include-internal include 2>/dev/null | grep -i "define\|= \|const" | head

content.text:
kappa == SEC_P_COM == 128, so bitlen is equal. Now the critical soundness analysis.

The PVE soundness relies on the cut-and-choose: verify checks that for each of `kappa` rows, the opened half is consistent with `Q` and the hash `b` binds the commitment. The unopened half (the ciphertext `c[i]` that decryption will use) is NOT directly verified to be correct — that's the nature of cut-and-choose. Soundness comes from: if any unopened ciphertext is malformed, decryption catches it via the `x_value * G == Q` check in `restore_from_decrypted`.

Let me carefully check the binding between what `verify` checks and what `decrypt` actually decrypts. In base `verify`, the verified row uses `xi` to recompute `c0[i]` and compares against the recomputed hash. But crucially: **does verify re-encrypt and check the ciphertext `c[i]` that decrypt will use?**

Let me trace the base case carefully. In `verify`, `c1[i] = c[i]` (the stored ciphertext, line 105). Then for `bi==1`, swap happens so `c0[i]` (recomputed from `xi`) and `c1[i]` (=c[i]) swap. The hash is over `c0,c1`. So `c[i]` IS included in the hash. Good — so the stored ciphertext is bound into `b`.

But the binding to plaintext: the hash binds `c[i]` (the unopened ciphertext bytes), but verify never decrypts `c[i]`. A malicious encryptor knows `dk`? No—encryptor only has `ek`. Let me think about the actual soundness theorem and whether the `x*G==Q` check fully saves it.

input.command: cd /c/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc && grep -rn "ec_pve_t\|ec_pve_batch_t\|ec_pve_ac_t" src/cbmpc/protocol/*.cpp include-internal | grep -iv "::" | head; echo "---api impl---"; grep -rln "namespace coinbase::api::pve\|api::pve" src | head

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. Let me reason about the core soundness binding carefully.

The decrypt path (skip_verify=true always in API) relies entirely on `restore_from_decrypted`'s final check `x_value * G == Q`. This is the actual soundness guarantee for *decryption correctness*: whatever is decrypted, if `x*G != Q`, it's rejected. So decryption cannot return a wrong nonzero plaintext silently — it would have to satisfy `x*G==Q`, meaning it IS the discrete log of Q.

Now the soundness of `verify` itself (the publicly-verifiable property): verify guarantees a holder of `dk` *will* be able to decrypt. This is the cut-and-choose. The concern: can verify pass but decryption fail for ALL rows (loss of escrow)? That requires all unopened ciphertexts to be malformed while all opened halves are correct — cut-and-choose makes this probability 2^-128. Standard.

Let me focus on a concrete binding gap. In the **batch verify**, note line 113: for `bi=1`, it re-encrypts `xi` to get `c1[i]`, and sets `c0[i] = rows[i].c` (the stored ct, which decryption uses). For `bi=0` (line 126), it re-encrypts `rows[i].r.take(16)` to get `c0[i]`, sets `c1[i] = rows[i].c`. The decryption uses `rows[i].c` always. 

Critical question for batch: in `restore_from_decrypted` when `bi=1`, the decrypted value `r01` (16 bytes) is used to regenerate `x0`, and `x1_bin` comes from stored `rows[i].x_bin`. The check `Q[i] == x[i]*G` for each i. This binds all n outputs to Q. Good.

Let me check one subtle thing: in batch verify `bi=1` branch, it checks `rows[i].x_bin.size() == n*curve_size` (line 108), but the `rows[i].c` (the stored ciphertext that decryption decrypts) when `bi=1` is supposed to be the encryption of `r01` (16 bytes). Verify does NOT re-derive or check what `rows[i].c` decrypts to — it only puts it into the hash. That's expected cut-and-choose. The decrypt-time check `r01.size != 16` (line 162) plus `x*G==Q` catches malformation.

Let me verify the `genPVELabelWithPoint` and the hash function bind correctly, and check `vector_from_bin` range behavior.

input.command: cd /c/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc && grep -rn "genPVELabelWithPoint\|hash_string" include-internal src/cbmpc/protocol/pve_base.cpp 2>/dev/null | head; echo ---; grep -rn "genPVELabelWithPoint" include-internal/cbmpc/internal/protocol/pve_base.h

content.text:
Now let me look very carefully at one specific soundness concern I want to rule in or out: the base `verify` reconstructs `X0[i] = xi*G` and `X1[i] = Q - X0[i]`, and the hash includes `Q, label, c0, c1, X0, X1`. The encryptor commits to `b = hash(...)`. Verify recomputes `b_tag` and checks equality.

The subtle point: `b` is stored by the encryptor (attacker-controlled, serialized). In verify, `b.get_bit(i)` decides which half to open. But `b` is also the hash output that must equal `b_tag`. Since `b_tag` is computed from `c0,c1,X0,X1` which depend on the `bi` choice (via swap), this is the Fiat-Shamir binding. An attacker who wants `b` to differ from `H(...)` will fail line 114. So `b` is effectively constrained = `H(transcript)`. Standard soundness.

The decryption's `x*G==Q` final check is the actual safety net. Both base and batch have it. This is sound.

Let me double check one thing in base decrypt: it loops over all kappa rows, calls `restore_from_decrypted`. For `bi=0` rows, the stored `c[i]=c0[i]` encrypts `x0`'s... wait. Let me re-trace base encrypt's storage of `c`.

In base encrypt line 55: `c_local[i] = bi ? c0[i] : c1[i]`. And `c0[i]` = encryption of `x0`, `c1[i]` = encryption of `x1`. For `bi=1`, stored c = c0 (encrypts x0); x_rows stores x1. For `bi=0`, stored c = c1 (encrypts x1); x_rows cleared (=0), r stored = r0.

In `restore_from_decrypted`: decrypted_x_buf is the decryption of stored c. For `bi=1`: `x_bi = x_rows[i]` (=x1), and `x_bi_bar` = decrypted = x0. Sum = x. For `bi=0`: `x_bi = drbg(r0).gen_bn` = x0, `x_bi_bar` = decrypted c1 = x1. Sum = x. Correct, and `x*G==Q` validates. Sound.

I've now thoroughly traced all received values. The construction's binding is solid: the final `x*G==Q` check in both `restore_from_decrypted` functions enforces that decryption returns exactly the discrete log of the verified `Q`, regardless of any ciphertext malformation. There is no soundness break reachable through the public API. This matches the first-pass result.

Let me do one final check — the `vector_from_bin` range enforcement and whether a malicious `b` of wrong bit-length could cause issues, and confirm `convert`/deser bounds.

input.command: cd /c/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc && grep -rn "bitlen\b\|get_bit\|is_in_range" include-internal/cbmpc/core/buf*.h include-internal/cbmpc/crypto/base.h 2>/dev/null | head; echo ---buf128---; grep -rn "class buf128_t\|get_bit\|bitlen" include-internal/cbmpc/core/*.h 2>/dev/null | head

content.text:
The buf128_t is fixed 128-bit, `get_bit(i)` for i in [0,128) is always valid (kappa=128). `b` being a buf128_t means it can't be wrong-length. The hash output `.bitlen(kappa)` truncates to 128 bits matching. All consistent.

My deep second pass confirms the first pass: no soundness break. The binding is enforced by the final discrete-log check at decryption. Returning zero findings honestly.

input.coverage_notes:
Deep second-pass audit of PVE verification soundness. Read in full: src/cbmpc/protocol/pve.cpp, pve_base.cpp, pve_batch.cpp; include/cbmpc/api/pve_base_pke.h, pve_batch_single_recipient.h; the API implementations src/cbmpc/api/pve_base_pke.cpp and pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp; and internal headers pve.h, pve_batch.h, pve_base.h. Result: ZERO findings. The construction's soundness binding is solid.

Core soundness conclusion: For both base (ec_pve_t) and batch (ec_pve_batch_t), the public decrypt/decrypt_batch APIs are always invoked with skip_verify=true and rely on the final discrete-log check in restore_from_decrypted: base pve.cpp:140 `if (x_value * G != Q) {x_value=0; return E_CRYPTO;}` and batch pve_batch.cpp:183 `if (Q[i] != x[i]*G) return E_CRYPTO;`. This check binds whatever bytes the base PKE decryption returns to the EXACT discrete log of the verified Q. A malicious encryptor therefore cannot make decryption silently return a different/zero/garbage scalar than committed: any deviation fails x*G==Q and is rejected (out_x set to 0 / out_xs cleared, error returned). No soundness break (wrong-plaintext acceptance) is reachable.

Received values I confirmed are properly validated:
- Q / Qs (caller-supplied expected point): on-curve via curve.check (pve.cpp:73, pve_batch.cpp:84); equals stored Q via `Q != this->Q` (pve.cpp:74, pve_batch.cpp:86); curve match enforced in API (pve_base_pke.cpp:233, pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:119-120); decoded from compressed bin with from_bin + check (pve_base_pke.cpp:236-237).
- label: matched against stored L (pve.cpp:75, pve_batch.cpp:87, and again in batch decrypt pve_batch.cpp:195); bound into inner_label via genPVELabelWithPoint (SHA-256 of point) and into the Fiat-Shamir hash.
- b (challenge / commitment): is a fixed-width buf128_t (kappa=SEC_P_COM=128), so get_bit(i) is always in-range; constrained to equal H(Q,label,c0,c1,X0,X1) via b_tag==b (pve.cpp:114, pve_batch.cpp:140). Encrypt uses .bitlen(kappa); batch verify uses .bitlen(SEC_P_COM) — confirmed equal (both 128), so no truncation mismatch.
- x_rows / x_bin (opened scalars): base bounds x_rows[i].get_bin_size()<=max_scalar_size and q.is_in_range (pve.cpp:88-89, 130-131); batch enforces rows[i].x_bin.size()==n*curve_size (pve_batch.cpp:108,159) and vector_from_bin range-checks against q.
- r (opened randomness): batch enforces exact sizes (16 for bi=1 at pve_batch.cpp:107; 32 for bi=0 at 117,165); base r is fixed buf128_t.
- decrypted_x_buf: size-checked before use (base pve.cpp:124 <=max_scalar_size, q.is_in_range:126; batch r01.size==16 at pve_batch.cpp:162, x1_bin.size==n*curve_size at 168).
- batch_count / n: bounded (0,MAX_BATCH_COUNT=100000] at parse (pve_batch_single_recipient.cpp:39-41) and re-checked in verify (pve_batch.cpp:77); Q.size()==n and Qs_compressed.size()==n enforced.
- stored ciphertext c[i]/rows[i].c: bound into the Fiat-Shamir hash (the unopened half is the cut-and-choose witness, deliberately not re-encrypted in verify); correctness of the unopened half is enforced at decrypt time by x*G==Q. Probability of verify-passing-but-all-rows-undecryptable is 2^-128 (standard cut-and-choose).
- API memory args: ek/dk/ciphertext size-bounded (MAX_OPAQUE_BLOB_SIZE / MAX_CIPHERTEXT_BLOB_SIZE); x/xs element size <= curve order size; version field checked ==1.
- key type / curve in base PKE bridge: validated (ECIES must be P-256), invalid key -> E_BADARG.

Deterministic-rho property (header contract pve_base_pke.h:11-15) is honored by the impl (pve_base.cpp:13 seeds drbg from rho) so verify's re-encryption reproduces the committed ciphertext bytes for the opened half; mismatch flips the hash and fails b_tag==b. No gap found.

content.text:
I completed a deep second-pass audit of the PVE verification soundness surface and found ZERO eligible vulnerabilities, which I report honestly.

The decisive reason there is no soundness break: although the public `decrypt`/`decrypt_batch` APIs always run with `skip_verify=true`, decryption does not trust the ciphertext blindly. Both `ec_pve_t::restore_from_decrypted` (pve.cpp:140) and `ec_pve_batch_t::restore_from_decrypted` (pve_batch.cpp:183) end with a hard `x*G == Q` check. That cryptographically binds whatever the base-PKE decryption returns to the exact discrete log of the verified `Q`. A malicious encryptor cannot craft a (ciphertext, proof) that passes `verify` yet decrypts to a different/zero/garbage scalar — any such deviation fails the discrete-log equality and is rejected.

I traced every received value (Q/Qs, label, challenge b, opened scalars x_rows/x_bin, opened randomness r, decrypted buffers, batch_count, ciphertext halves, and all API memory args) to its validation site; details and evidence are in the coverage notes. The cut-and-choose challenge `b` is correctly Fiat-Shamir-bound (`b_tag == b`), is a fixed 128-bit value (no length manipulation), and the encrypt/verify hash bit-lengths are equal (kappa == SEC_P_COM == 128). No missing check exploitable by a malicious party against honest counterparties on unmodified code.
